summary: Jeff Atwood uses the Pontiac Aztek as a cautionary tale about design by committee, illustrating how a promising concept can be destroyed by too many compromises. The Aztek was actually ahead of its time as a crossover SUV, but committee decisions, cost-cutting, and the requirement to use an existing minivan platform produced a notoriously ugly vehicle. Atwood connects this to software development through Don Norman's observation that good design requires a dictator, not a committee. The post notes that Steve McConnell references the Aztek five times in Code Complete 2 as shorthand for compromised design. The core message is that quality must be planned from the beginning—you can't test your way from an Aztek to a Rolls-Royce.
key_points: Design by committee produces least-common-denominator results because everyone's input leads to endless compromises
The Pontiac Aztek was actually a good concept (crossover SUV) that arrived before the trend, but poor execution killed it
Cost-cutting decisions like using an existing minivan platform destroyed the original design proportions
Good software design requires a coherent vision from a single authority, not democratic consensus
Quality must be designed in from the start—testing cannot transform a fundamentally compromised design into excellence
money_quotes: You don't do good software design by committee. You do it best by having a dictator.
By the time it was done, it came out as this horrible, least-common-denominator vehicle where everyone said, 'How could you put that on the road?'
If you start the process with designs for a Pontiac Aztek, you can test it all you want to, and it will never turn into a Rolls-Royce.
Design would be by committee, the focus groups extensive. And production would have to stick to a tight budget, with all that sex appeal packed onto an existing minivan platform.
It looks the way Montezuma's revenge feels.
The Aztek is uniquely American. And too often, so is the least common denominator result of design by committee.
key_insight: Coherent design requires unified vision and authority; committee-driven compromise transforms promising concepts into lowest-common-denominator failures that no amount of testing can redeem.
